artifacts.content:
Hi Mam,

Finally grabbing ten quiet minutes while the girls are at piano and Emily is marking essays. I wanted to explain properly what’s been going on, because you’ll probably see the bank letters if you’re over here and I don’t want you worrying without the full picture.

The main thing is the mortgage has slipped into arrears over the last 8 months. Between Sophie’s asthma costs, the bonus being cut at Emerald Tech, and everything going up, we’ve been juggling more than we should. You know the house here at 18 Willow Park Drive is our anchor, and I’m determined we’ll keep it, but the full repayment of €1,425.60 has been hard to hit every single month while we also clear the backlog on the credit card.

We’re not burying our heads. I’ve tightened the budget, cancelled the gym, and even renegotiated broadband and mobiles. My preferred contact is mobile now – the (353) 087-2345 number – because I’m in and out between client sites and home. I’m also doing a bit of evening freelance project work for Horizon Systems in the Docklands to bring in extra.

I’ve all the mortgage bits written down so I can keep track: the account is MTG-2020-458392, current balance about €238,500.75 on a place now worth roughly €395,000. Seeing those numbers in black and white is a bit sobering, but it also reminds me why we’re fighting so hard to protect the house for Aoife and Sophie.

Anyway, don’t panic – we’re stressed but we’re okay, and Emily and I are pulling the same direction. I’ll fill you in more when we see you Sunday.

Love,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Bit more detail on the house and money

artifacts.content:
Hi Emily,

Just out of that HR meeting at the Emerald Tech Dublin office and I need to get this down while it’s still fresh in my head.

They’ve confirmed they’re terminating my employment, effective today, 12 March 2024, over the data-handling error on the Health Service Executive (HSE) e‑procurement rollout. They’re calling it “gross misconduct” because of how a test data set with supplier details was handled during the final deployment weekend in the city-centre war room. I kept saying it was a process failure across the team, but the internal disciplinary panel clearly decided someone’s head had to roll and, as Senior Project Manager, it’s mine.

I’m numb, to be honest. One minute I’m worrying about a steering committee pack, the next I’m being walked out of the HSE project floor with my laptop in a cardboard box. Patrick from HR kept repeating that I can say I’m “between roles” when I’m talking to recruiters, but we both know this is going to be awkward to explain.

Practically, we’ll have to rethink everything – the mortgage on Willow Park, the girls’ activities, even your hours at St. Brigid’s. I’m grateful you’re permanent part‑time there now, but losing my Emerald Tech salary and what was left of the bonus potential is a huge hit. I’ll call Horizon Systems and anyone else I know about freelance contracts as soon as I calm down.

I’ll be on the DART back to Blackrock in about 20 minutes. Can we talk properly once the girls are in bed? For now, just tell them Daddy’s working from home for a while.

D.

Personal note – work/health timeline (for my own head)

I keep getting tangled trying to explain the last few years, so here’s the rough sequence.

I joined Emerald Tech Consulting Ltd. in March 2019 as Senior Project Manager and, on paper, I’ve had 7 years’ continuous service there. The only weird patch was May–September 2020, when Patrick logged me as “flexible working” so I could work reduced hours from home in Blackrock. Schools were closed in the first COVID lockdown, Aoife and Sophie were both here 24/7, and Emily was shielding because of her asthma. I was basically half‑PM, half‑primary school teacher from the box room at 18 Willow Park Drive.

Things ramped up again from January 2021 to April 2024 when I led that high‑pressure IT transformation for St. Brigid’s Community College – Emily’s school. That project just never stopped: weekend cutovers, endless change requests, and constant politics because some of her colleagues didn’t like that her husband was running “their” system. By early 2024 I was waking up at 3 a.m. worrying about exam timetables and user permissions.

In May 2024, after months of feeling wired and exhausted, I finally saw Dr. Fiona Byrne at Blackrock Medical Centre. She listened to the whole Emerald/St. Brigid’s saga and called it what it is: generalized anxiety disorder, driven by chronic stress. It was weirdly a relief to have a name for it, but also scary to realise how long I’ve been running on fumes.

Need to remember: none of this is a moral failure, just too many plates spinning for too long.

Hi Sean,

Long overdue update from the Blackrock chaos.

Work-wise, I’m technically “between roles” now. I wrapped up at Emerald Tech at the end of February 2024 – my last big piece there was a €3.2 million security‑cleared implementation for the UK Home Office in London. Our main contact was a guy called Mark Ellison; nice enough, but every meeting felt like an exam. I was back and forth between the Dublin office and their London HQ for months.

That pressure, plus the St. Brigid’s Community College transformation I’d been running since January 2021, was brutal. Trying to be the calm Senior PM on a UK government job while also firefighting Emily’s school’s IT mess nearly cooked me. By the time we hit April 2024, I was completely fried.

In the middle of all that, the money side at home tightened. You remember our place – 18 Willow Park Drive – mortgage is still about €238.5k on a house worth roughly €395k. The full €1,425‑ish monthly repayment is grand when the bonus comes through, but last year Emerald quietly reduced the bonus structure and we were already leaning on overdraft and cards. Then Sophie’s asthma kicked off and we had a run of hospital and GP bills.

I finally went to Blackrock Medical Centre and got a proper diagnosis of generalized anxiety disorder from a GP, which weirdly helped. I’ve since taken on some freelance project‑planning work for Horizon Systems in the Docklands – smaller stuff, no HSE or Home Office drama – and trying to be more present with the girls.

Anyway, that’s my novella. How’s London treating you? Still surviving the Tube?

Cheers,
Dan

metadata.subject: Life after Emerald / Home Office saga

artifacts.content:
Money + arrears – running list so I don’t forget the sequence

1) Revenue underpayment – mid‑Jan 2024
On 10 January 2024, Laura Byrne in Emerald Tech HR forwarded the revised tax calc from Revenue Commissioners about the 2022–2023 bonus coding error. We owed €1,750. I panicked about anything hanging over us and paid it in one lump mid‑January. Result: we only managed about 60% of the January mortgage instalment.

2) Horizon Systems invoice delay – March/April 2024
Took a short evening contract for Horizon Systems Ltd. in their Dublin Docklands office, counting on the €1,200 invoice to plug the gap for March and April repayments. Mark O’Shea in finance sat on the approval for six weeks. We ended up skipping roughly half of the March mortgage and never fully caught up in April.

3) Emily’s hours at St. Brigid’s – Feb/March 2024
In November 2023 her timetable was bumped up to cover Ms. Kelly’s stress leave, so we built the 2024 budget assuming those extra 8–10 hours a week would stick. On 5 February 2024 the St. Brigid’s board confirmed Ms. Kelly’s return and Emily dropped back to strictly part‑time. That’s about €620 less per month than we’d planned, which is exactly the wiggle room we used to have for arrears catch‑up.

Add on Sophie’s asthma costs, higher utilities, and normal stuff like car insurance and school books, and it’s obvious how we slid into arrears even while cutting back on everything else.

metadata.title: Arrears – key hits on cash flow

artifacts.content:
Hi Mary,

Just wanted to fill you in a bit more on Sophie and why money’s been so tight since the summer.

In late August 2024 we had a run of urgent asthma appointments for her at the respiratory clinic in Our Lady’s Children’s Hospital in Crumlin. She saw her consultant, Dr. Keane, twice in the space of about ten days after that scary night when she was wheezing and the inhaler wasn’t touching it. On top of the consultations, we had to buy a home nebuliser and a couple of spacer devices – the lot came to just over €480 before we even got to the usual GP bills.

All of that landed right as we were paying for back‑to‑school bits for both girls – uniforms, books, camogie fees for Aoife. When the invoice from Crumlin came in on 2 September 2024, we decided to clear it in full rather than risk any delay with the hospital, which meant we couldn’t make the full mortgage repayment that week. That’s when the arrears really started to build instead of just being a one‑off short month.

We’ve since tried to cushion things a bit with a tiny emergency fund so the next car service or school trip doesn’t knock us sideways, and I’m doing more evening work to bring in extra. But between Sophie’s ongoing asthma checks, Emily’s hours at St. Brigid’s being back to strict part‑time, and everything from groceries to gas going up, it’s been a slog.

The main thing is Sophie’s breathing is better now that we’ve got the nebuliser routine down, and the house is still very much our home. We’re just having to be more organised than ever.

Talk soon,
Daniel

Event: Weekly planning + money check‑in with Emily
Date: Sunday 2024-11-17
Time: 8:30 p.m. – 10:00 p.m.
Location: Kitchen table, 18 Willow Park Drive, Blackrock
Attendees: Daniel, Emily

Agenda / notes:

– Quick debrief on week: Emily’s timetable at St. Brigid’s Community College (still part‑time since Ms. Kelly returned), girls’ school stuff, Sophie’s asthma (check if any follow‑up at Our Lady’s Crumlin with Dr. Keane before Christmas).

– Finances:
  • Review current mortgage situation on MTG-2020-458392 – outstanding balance approx. €238,500.75; property value still around €395,000.
  • Confirm November repayment – target is full €1,425.60 this month plus something small towards arrears if possible.
  • Update shared spreadsheet with latest utilities (post‑fixed‑rate jump), groceries, and fuel.
  • Check emergency fund status – aim to keep at least one car service and a school trip buffer there so we’re not leaning on overdraft.

– Income:
  • Daniel: status of freelance project‑planning work for Horizon Systems (Docklands) and any new leads from ex‑Emerald Tech contacts.
  • Emily: any talk at school about extra hours next term; impact on childcare/school runs if that happens.

– Health / headspace:
  • Daniel: how anxiety has been this week; whether to book another check‑in with Dr. Fiona Byrne at Blackrock Medical Centre.
  • Emily: marking load and energy levels.

– Finish with 10‑minute chat about Christmas plans so life isn’t all numbers.

metadata.title: Weekly budget + life check‑in
metadata.location: 18 Willow Park Drive, Blackrock, Co. Dublin, A94 F2K3, Ireland

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel O'Connor <daniel.oconnor83@examplemail.ie>
To: Mam <mary.oconnor@examplemail.ie>
Date: 15 June 2023
Subject: Aoife, work, and money

Hi Mam,

I wanted to explain properly what’s happening before you hear bits and pieces from Emily or the girls.

I’ve agreed with HR at the Emerald Tech Consulting Ltd. Dublin office to take a three‑month unpaid leave from July to September 2023 so I can be with Aoife full‑time for her surgery and recovery at Our Lady’s Children’s Hospital, Crumlin. The dates aren’t ideal from a money point of view, but with everything the consultants have said, I don’t want Emily trying to juggle the hospital, Sophie, and her timetable at St. Brigid’s on her own.

You know I’ve been a Senior Project Manager at Emerald Tech Consulting Ltd. since June 2013, and normally the bonus and overtime keep us comfortable enough. But because this is completely unpaid, we’ve had to admit we’re going to fall behind on the mortgage for a bit. The repayments on 18 Willow Park Drive are tight even in a normal month, and losing three full paycheques is going to push us into arrears unless the bank agrees to something temporary.

I’ve spoken to the lender’s arrears support team and they said once the leave is confirmed in writing, we can apply for an interest‑only arrangement for those three months. I’m drafting a letter explaining that the unpaid caregiving leave is the reason we’re struggling.

Please don’t worry Emily with questions about money when you see her – she’s already anxious about Aoife’s surgery. If you can help with Sophie on the school‑run days, that would be the biggest gift.

Love,
Daniel

From: Daniel O'Connor <daniel.oconnor83@examplemail.ie>
To: Arrears Support Unit <home.loans@greenislebank.ie>
Date: 12 February 2024
Subject: Mortgage arrears – explanation of change in circumstances (Account: O’Connor – 18 Willow Park Drive, Blackrock, A94 F2K3)

Dear Arrears Support Team,

I am writing to explain the circumstances that led to our recent mortgage arrears on the above property.

I, Daniel O’Connor (borrower), am currently not working. Until recently I was employed as a Senior Project Manager at Emerald Tech Consulting Ltd. (Dublin office) from June 2013 to January 2024, overseeing large‑scale software implementation projects for corporate clients. Due to a combination of family health issues and restructuring at Emerald Tech, my employment ended in January 2024 and I am now actively seeking new work.

A key factor in our financial difficulties was a three‑month unpaid leave I had to take from July to September 2023. Our daughter, Aoife O’Connor, required surgery and follow‑up treatment at Our Lady’s Children’s Hospital, Crumlin, and I became her primary caregiver during this period. With Emily working part‑time as a teacher and also caring for our younger daughter, Sophie, it was not feasible for me to continue full‑time work.

This unpaid caregiving leave resulted in the loss of three months’ income and directly caused us to fall behind on the mortgage. We used overdraft and credit facilities to cover essential living and medical expenses, but despite our best efforts we entered arrears.

We are committed to keeping our home at 18 Willow Park Drive and are requesting a temporary alternative repayment arrangement while I secure new employment.

Kind regards,

Daniel O’Connor
18 Willow Park Drive
Blackrock, Co. Dublin, A94 F2K3
Mobile: +353 87 2345
Email: daniel.oconnor83@examplemail.ie
